my_thoughts: The validation/hierarchy works: tramp = irresponsibility (definition correct), identifies: common phrases ('throw everything overboard'), asserts: relative danger (lunatic > tramp), prioritizes: lunatic danger (more dangerous). The student proposed archetypal definition (seeking archetypal definition), which shows they understand: tramp = irresponsibility (archetypal definition). They'll likely accept the validation/hierarchy. The episode is complete: definition proposal → functional definition → archetypal → validation/hierarchy. Pattern complete.
accumulated_insight: Two-turn pattern: T1: Student proposed definition (seeking functional definition)—functional definition: lunatic = false values + formatory thinking. T2: Student proposed archetypal definition ('Is the tramp side in oneself a sort of irresponsibility that is prepared to throw everything overboard?')—seeking archetypal definition via proposes definition and seeks confirmation. They understand: tramp = irresponsibility (archetypal definition). The comparison_contrast mechanism validates: tramp = irresponsibility (definition correct), identifies: common phrases ('throw everything overboard'), asserts: relative danger (lunatic > tramp), prioritizes: lunatic danger (more dangerous). Pattern complete: definition proposal → functional definition → archetypal question → validation/hierarchy. They've shifted: tramp = irresponsibility (validated), lunatic > tramp (danger hierarchy) (validation/hierarchy).
visible_response:
Quite right.
Sometimes it can take very poetical forms: 'There are no values in the
world'—'Nothing is worth anything'—'Everything is relative'—those are favourite
phrases.
But actually the tramp is not so dangerous. The lunatic is more dangerous,
since it means false values and formatory thinking.
